request: We'd like managers to edit and re-approve documents after they've already been signed.

problem.value: Managers likely need to correct errors or update terms in documents that were already signed, without losing legal traceability of the original signature
problem.evidence: Request: 'edit and re-approve documents after they've already been signed'

current_process.value: Unknown — possibly a manual workaround today (print, edit outside system, re-sign) or simply not possible yet

actors.value: Managers trigger the edit and the re-approval; unclear whether they are also the approver, or whether original signatories/other roles must re-approve

business_objects.value: Document, Version, Approval/Signatory entities are involved, per the platform's Document/Version model
business_objects.evidence: Product context: Document vs Version, Approval circuit

business_rules.value: A signed document is frozen; 'editing' it almost certainly means creating a new version, not patching content in place. Whether re-approval repeats the full original circuit or a lighter one, and whether this varies by document type/classification, is undefined
business_rules.evidence: Context card: 'a later edit is a new version, never an in-place change'

workflow.value: Likely lifecycle: signed doc -> manager opens new draft version -> edits -> submits -> approval circuit runs again -> new version approved/signed -> supersedes prior version (prior retained, referenceable)

integrations.value: Likely need to notify prior approvers/readers that a new version exists and is pending or has replaced the old one
integrations.evidence: Not stated; inferred from notification-on-event pattern

permissions.value: Managers can edit signed documents - but which managers (any manager, only the document's owner/approver, scoped by classification or folder) is unspecified, and who re-approves is unclear

config_vs_custom.value: Unknown whether this re-approval rule should be uniform, or configurable per client/document type/classification

constraints.value: Prior signed version must remain retained and referenceable for legal/audit validity; e-signature validity rules likely require re-collecting signatures on the new version, not silently overwriting
constraints.evidence: Context: frozen-after-approval rule, e-signature validity, audit trail as hard requirement

edge_cases.value: What happens to the currently published version while the edited one is under re-approval? Are links/citations to the old signed version reconciled? What if the document is already superseded or archived under retention?

reporting.value: An audit trail showing who edited, who re-approved, previous vs new version, and timestamps is very likely mandatory, not optional
reporting.evidence: Context: 'an audit trail is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have - retrofitting it late is expensive'

risks.value: Risk of confusing 'approved' with 'live/current' if the old signed version stays published while the new one is under re-approval; risk of breaking prior signature validity or references to the old version

questions.q: When a manager edits a signed document, should this create a new version (old one retained and referenceable, prior signature preserved) rather than modifying the signed content in place?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: the whole build differs between 'new version + re-approval' (versioning, supersession, audit trail) and 'patch the signed doc' (which breaks signature validity and audit integrity) - this is the core architectural decision

questions.q: Does re-approval mean the full original approval/signature circuit runs again, or a lighter review - and does this change by document type or classification (e.g. confidential vs internal)?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: determines whether we reuse the existing multi-step circuit engine as-is or need a distinct 're-approval' path, and whether it's configurable per client/document type

questions.q: Which managers can trigger this - any manager, only the document owner, or only someone with a specific role/classification clearance - and who must re-approve (same approvers as before, or could differ)?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: permissions on signed/classified documents are high-blast-radius; getting scope wrong here is a classic acceptance-testing failure per the platform's own traps

questions.q: While the edited version is going through re-approval, does the previously signed version stay published/current, or should it be flagged/withdrawn until the new one is approved?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: this is exactly the 'approved != published/current' trap - getting it wrong means people act on the wrong version during the re-approval window

questions.q: Is there a regulatory/compliance driver behind this (e.g. contract amendments, audited policies) that requires a specific audit trail format, or is this an internal convenience feature?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: a named compliance obligation would make the audit trail, permissions and constraints all high-cost, non-negotiable build items rather than a generic activity log

summary.objective: Allow managers to correct or update a document that has already been signed, while re-establishing valid approval/signature on the corrected content.
summary.scope: Likely involves: creating a new document Version from the signed one (content editable only in the new version, prior version frozen and retained), re-running an approval/signature circuit on that new version, then supersession of the old version once the new one is approved - plus an audit trail linking old and new versions, who edited, who re-approved, and when.
assumptions: 'Edit' means creating a new version, not modifying the signed content in place
Re-approval re-triggers an approval/signature circuit similar to the original one
The previously signed version remains retained and referenceable after being superseded
An audit trail of the edit/re-approval is required, given the platform's compliance-driven audit trail standard
Only managers (not arbitrary users) can initiate this edit, though the exact scope of 'manager' is unconfirmed
summary.blind_spot: The request sounds like a simple 'unlock and edit' feature, but it collides directly with the platform's frozen-after-approval rule: a signed document cannot be edited in place without invalidating its legal/audit value. The real build is version supersession + a defined re-approval circuit + careful handling of what happens to the 'live' document during the re-approval window - none of which is mentioned in the request.

problem.value: Managers need to correct or update documents whose content is already signed off — likely error correction or mandated change, not casual editing.
problem.evidence: "edit and re-approve documents after they've already been signed"

actors.value: Managers trigger edit + re-approval. Unclear whether original signatories/approvers must also re-approve or are just notified.

business_objects.value: Document, Version, Approval, Signatory — standard platform model applies.
business_objects.evidence: context: Document/Version/Approval are the core entities

business_rules.value: Editing a signed document should create a new Version (supersession), not an in-place change; prior version retained and referenceable.
business_rules.evidence: context trap: "edit a signed/approved document" hides supersession

workflow.value: Likely: new version created as draft → re-enters approval circuit → approved; interaction with the separate publication lifecycle (is the old published version still 'current' while the new one is pending re-approval?) is undefined.
workflow.evidence: context: two independent lifecycles (approval vs publication) must not be conflated

permissions.value: Managers can initiate edit + re-approval, but composition of the re-approval circuit (same signatories vs new ones) and who else may act during pending re-approval is unspecified.
permissions.evidence: context: "the identity of the re-approver after an edit is rarely the same as the original"

config_vs_custom.value: Unclear if re-approval behavior (circuit, who may trigger, which document types) is configurable per client/document type or a single global rule.
config_vs_custom.evidence: context: approval circuits and classification schemes are client-specific

constraints.value: Electronic-signature validity and retention obligations likely constrain how re-signature/re-approval is handled.

edge_cases.value: What happens to prior signatures (invalidated vs re-collected), existing links/citations to the old version, and documents under legal hold?
edge_cases.evidence: context: prior signatures invalidated or re-collected; legal hold can freeze disposition

reporting.value: An audit trail (who edited, who re-approved, previous vs new value, timestamps) is assumed mandatory given the compliance nature of signed documents.
reporting.evidence: context: audit trail is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have, in a signed-document workflow

risks.value: Risk of users acting on a stale/superseded version during the re-approval window if publication status isn't clearly managed.

questions.q: When a manager edits a signed document, does this create a new Version that must go through its own draft → review → approval cycle (with the old version retained and stamped superseded), or is an in-place edit of the signed content actually expected?
questions.why: high impact / low completeness: this decides whether we build versioning+supersession or a much riskier direct mutation of signed content; it also determines what happens to the original signatures and any links pointing at the old version.

questions.q: During the time the new version is being re-approved, what should users see and act on — the still-published old (signed) version, a 'pending re-approval' marker, or nothing until the new version is approved?
questions.why: high impact / inferred only: approval and publication are separate lifecycles here; without this answer we risk people acting on the wrong version, a named trap in this domain.

questions.q: Who must re-approve the edited document — the same signatories/approvers as the original signed version, a role-based circuit that could differ, or can the manager configure this per document type/client?
questions.why: high impact / inferred: re-approver identity is rarely the same as the original per domain notes, and this also decides if config_vs_custom work is needed.

questions.q: Is a full audit trail (who edited what, previous vs new content, who re-approved, when) a hard compliance requirement for this document type, or is it just informal traceability?
questions.why: high impact / inferred: audit trail is flagged as a hard requirement in this domain; confirming scope now avoids an expensive retrofit.

questions.q: What happens to the original signatures and to anything (other documents, external references) pointing at the now-superseded version — must signatures be re-collected, and do references need updating?
questions.why: high impact / empty: unresolved, this is a classic source of legal/compliance gaps and broken references after supersession.

summary.objective: Allow managers to modify the content of an already-signed document and have it re-validated, without breaking the integrity of the original signed record.
summary.scope: Likely: editing a signed document creates a new Version (draft), which re-enters an approval/signature circuit; the prior signed version is retained and marked superseded; publication status is managed independently so users don't act on the wrong version; an audit trail records the edit and re-approval.
assumptions: Editing produces a new Version rather than mutating the signed content in place (supersession pattern).
The re-approval circuit may reuse or differ from the original signatories.
Publication and approval lifecycles remain distinct — the old version may stay 'current' until the new one is approved and published.
An audit trail of the edit and re-approval is required for compliance.
Behavior (who can trigger edits, circuit composition) may need to be configurable per client/document type.
summary.blind_spot: The request sounds like a simple 'edit + re-approve' toggle, but it actually hides version supersession, the fate of prior signatures and external references, and the ambiguity between 'approved' and 'published/current' — any of which, if unaddressed, lets someone act on a stale or invalid version of the document.

problem.value: Managers need a way to correct or update content in documents that have already been signed, without losing legal validity — likely driven by errors found post-signature or a periodic revision need.
problem.evidence: Request states 'edit and re-approve documents after they've already been signed' but gives no root cause (error fix vs. scheduled revision).

current_process.value: Platform default: once a version is approved/signed it is frozen; there is no sanctioned in-app path today to change it — managers likely work around this outside the tool.
current_process.evidence: Context card: 'a version is frozen once approved/signed... a later edit is a new version, never an in-place change.'

actors.value: Managers edit and trigger re-approval. Unclear whether managers are also the re-approvers, or whether a separate approval circuit re-validates the new version.
actors.evidence: 'managers to edit and re-approve' — explicit on the editor role, silent on who re-approves.

business_objects.value: Document, Version, Approval, Signatory — standard GED model likely reused: editing a signed doc should create a new Version rather than mutate the signed one.
business_objects.evidence: Context card domain notes on Document vs Version and the frozen-after-approval rule.

business_rules.value: Unclear who exactly may re-open a signed document for edit (any manager vs. document owner only), whether this applies to all document types/classifications, and whether prior signatures are invalidated or must be re-collected.
business_rules.evidence: Context card trap: 'edit a signed/approved document hides supersession... prior signatures invalidated or re-collected.'

workflow.value: Likely: manager edits → creates new draft version → submits to approval circuit → approved/signed → new version becomes current, old version stamped superseded (or archived per retention). Exact trigger and state names unconfirmed.
workflow.evidence: Context card: 'Document vs Version' pattern — 'edit' means 'supersede with a new version'.

integrations.value: Unclear who must be notified when a signed document is reopened/re-approved (original signatories? readers of the prior version? downstream systems referencing the old version?).
integrations.evidence: Context card trap: 'notify on a document implies which event and which role/classification may even be told it exists.'

permissions.value: Unclear whether all managers can reopen any signed document, or only within their scope/classification; also unclear who can see the superseded version vs. only the new one.
permissions.evidence: Context card: classification drives who may read/edit; permissions is a recurring blind spot ('forgotten until acceptance testing').

config_vs_custom.value: Likely standard-for-all mechanism (versioning/supersession is core), but the re-approval circuit composition may be client-specific.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context card: 'Standard for all: frozen-after-approval rule... Client-specific: approval circuits.'

edge_cases.value: What happens to documents already published/distributed from the signed version while a new one is under re-approval? What if re-approval is rejected — does the old signed version remain current?
edge_cases.evidence: Context card: two independent lifecycles (approval vs publication) — a document can be published then superseded.

reporting.value: An audit trail of who edited, who re-approved, previous vs. new value, and version history is almost certainly mandatory given the compliance nature of signed documents.
reporting.evidence: Context card: 'an audit trail... is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have — retrofitting it late is expensive.'

risks.value: Risk of users acting on the wrong version during the re-approval window (old signed version still 'current' while new one pending), and risk of confusing 'approved' with 'published/current'.

questions.q: When a manager edits a signed document, must the prior signed version be preserved and clearly marked superseded (versioning), or is the expectation to actually overwrite/replace the signed content in place?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred) × high impact: this decides between a full new-version/supersession build (audit-safe) vs. a much riskier in-place edit — the two are fundamentally different implementations and the request doesn't say which.

questions.q: Who is authorized to reopen a signed document for editing — any manager, only the document owner/author, or scoped by classification/department — and does re-approval require the same signatories as before or a new circuit?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) × high impact: defines the permission model and approval circuit; getting this wrong creates a compliance/security gap.

questions.q: Is a full audit trail required (who edited what, previous vs. new content, who re-approved, when) for compliance/legal defensibility of the re-approved document?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred) × high impact: signed documents are typically compliance-sensitive; an audit trail retrofitted late is expensive, so this must be settled before build.

questions.q: While the edited version is going through re-approval, does the original signed version remain the 'current/published' one, or is it immediately taken out of circulation?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) × high impact: this is the classic 'approved ≠ published/current' trap — determines what readers see during the re-approval window and whether stale actions can occur.

questions.q: Should the original signatories (and/or readers of the old version) be notified when the document is reopened, and again when it's re-approved — and by which channel?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) × medium impact: notification routing affects who might unknowingly rely on outdated content during the transition.

summary.objective: Allow managers to modify the content of an already-signed document and have it go through re-approval, while preserving the legal/compliance integrity of the original signed record.
summary.scope: Likely: a new Version is created from the signed Document (not an in-place edit), routed through an approval circuit for re-signature; the old version is retained and stamped superseded; audit trail captures the change and re-approval history; permissions gate who can trigger this reopening.
assumptions: Editing a signed document means creating a new version, not mutating the signed one (per platform's frozen-after-approval rule).
Managers are the ones initiating the edit, but re-approval likely still runs through a defined approval circuit, not just the manager rubber-stamping their own edit.
An audit trail of edits and re-approvals is required given the compliance sensitivity of signed documents.
The old signed version remains referenceable/superseded rather than deleted.
summary.blind_spot: The request frames this as a simple 'edit + re-approve' action, but it actually collides with two of the platform's core rules: (1) a signed version is frozen — 'editing' it really means superseding it with a new version, and (2) approval and publication are separate lifecycles, so there's an ambiguous window where the old signed version may still be 'current' while the new one awaits re-approval. Without clarifying who can trigger this, what happens to the old version during that window, and whether the change is audit-logged, the team risks building a simple overwrite that breaks traceability and creates a period where nobody is sure which version is authoritative.
